---
title: "Dataset and Preprocessing"
---

# Dataset and Preprocessing

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary
We trained and evaluated the system on MedSynth — a carefully constructed
synthetic dataset of 10,240 clinical notes, each paired with a verified
ICD-10 code. This section explains where the data came from, how it was
cleaned, and how we structured it to prevent the model from "cheating" by
finding shortcuts in the text.
:::

---

## The MedSynth Dataset {#sec-data-medsynth}

MedSynth [@Ahmad2025] is a publicly available synthetic clinical NLP dataset
hosted on HuggingFace. It was generated by prompting GPT-4o to write
realistic clinical notes for each ICD-10 code in its scope, producing
**exactly 5 notes per code** — a deliberately uniform distribution chosen
to ensure representation across the full code set.

The dataset contains **10,240 records** in total. After filtering to
billable codes only (removing placeholder codes, unspecified codes, and codes
that require combination coding), **9,660 records** remain across **1,926
ICD-10 codes**.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Total records | 10,240 |
| Billable records (post-filter) | 9,660 |
| ICD-10 codes covered | 1,926 |
| Records per code (uniform) | ~5 |
| Source model | GPT-4o |
| Note format | SOAP (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan) |
| Mean note length | ~380 words |
| Note length range | 87 – 1,300+ words |

: MedSynth dataset statistics. {#tbl-data-stats}

### Strengths

MedSynth has several properties that make it well-suited for this research:

- **Uniform code coverage.** With 5 examples per code, every ICD-10 code
  in scope has representation in the training set. Real clinical datasets
  have highly skewed distributions where common codes (hypertension,
  diabetes) have thousands of examples and rare codes have one or two.
- **Clean label assignment.** Each note was generated specifically for a
  single ICD-10 code, so label assignment is unambiguous.
- **Structured format.** All notes follow the SOAP format, enabling
  consistent preprocessing.
- **No protected health information.** Synthetic data eliminates PHI
  concerns entirely.

### Limitations

The uniform distribution is also MedSynth's primary limitation: it does not
reflect real clinical practice, where code frequencies span many orders of
magnitude. A model trained on MedSynth will not have seen a realistic
distribution of conditions and may not generalise to real-world coding where
a handful of codes dominate the workload.

Additionally, GPT-4o-generated notes may exhibit stylistic patterns —
sentence structure, vocabulary choices, level of clinical detail — that
differ systematically from notes written by real clinicians. This introduces
a synthetic-to-real generalisation gap that cannot be quantified without
access to real clinical data.

---

## Label Validation {#sec-data-validation}

Not all ICD-10 codes in MedSynth are appropriate targets for this project.
We applied a three-stage validation pipeline to clean the label space.

**Stage 1 — Billable filter.** ICD-10 codes are classified as billable
(acceptable for claim submission as a primary diagnosis), placeholder
(administrative codes not valid as primary diagnoses), or noisy (codes
flagged in MedSynth as potentially misassigned). We retain only billable
codes.

**Stage 2 — CDC FY2026 validation.** We cross-reference each retained code
against the official CDC ICD-10-CM FY2026 release to confirm it is a valid,
current code. Codes that do not appear in the current release are flagged.

**Stage 3 — Post-publication code removal.** MedSynth was generated before
the FY2026 code update. A small number of codes that were valid at generation
time were subsequently retired or restructured. These are identified and
excluded.

| Filter stage | Records removed | Records remaining |
|---|---|---|
| Raw dataset | — | 10,240 |
| Billable filter | 555 noisy + 25 placeholder | 9,660 |
| CDC FY2026 validation | 14 invalid codes | 9,590 |
| Post-publication removal | 12 retired codes | 9,578 |

: Label validation pipeline. {#tbl-data-validation}

The final validated dataset of 9,578 billable records across 1,914 codes
is used for all training and evaluation. For consistency with the experimental
progression, results in this document are reported against the 9,660-record
pre-CDC-validation split, which was used for all model training.

---

## APSO-Flip Preprocessing {#sec-data-apso}

The single most important preprocessing decision in this project is
**APSO-Flip** — a reordering of the SOAP note structure before tokenisation.

### The Problem: Context Window Truncation

Bio_ClinicalBERT has a maximum context window of **512 tokens** — approximately
400 words. Many MedSynth notes exceed this length. When a note is truncated,
content at the end is silently dropped.

In a SOAP-format note, the sections appear in the order:

```
Subjective  → Patient's reported symptoms
Objective   → Examination findings and vital signs
Assessment  → Physician's diagnosis
Plan        → Treatment and follow-up
```

The **Assessment** section — which contains the most diagnostically
informative content — typically appears in the third or fourth position,
often beyond the 512-token boundary in long notes. A model that only sees
the first 512 tokens of a long SOAP note may never encounter the diagnosis.

### The Solution: Reorder to APSO


APSO-Flip reorders the sections so Assessment appears first:

```
Assessment  → Physician's diagnosis         ← most informative
Plan        → Treatment and follow-up       ← still informative
Subjective  → Patient's reported symptoms   ← context
Objective   → Examination findings          ← context
```

![Token length distributions for clinical notes (blue) and full SOAP dialogue (green) against the Bio_ClinicalBERT 512-token context window limit (red dashed line). The majority of notes already approach or exceed the limit — APSO-Flip ensures the most diagnostically informative content (Assessment) is never truncated.](images/Token_pressure_vs_context_window.png){#fig-token-pressure fig-alt="KDE plot showing note token distribution peaking near the 512 token limit"}


This guarantees that the most diagnostically informative content falls within
the first 512 tokens regardless of note length. The reordering is applied at
both training and inference time.

::: {.callout-important}
## Why APSO-Flip Matters

On notes with more than 400 words, APSO-Flip is the difference between the
model seeing the diagnosis ("Assessment: Iron deficiency anaemia") and only
seeing the symptom description ("Subjective: Patient reports fatigue and
pallor for the past three weeks"). Without APSO-Flip, the model is asked to
infer the code from incomplete evidence on a substantial fraction of the
training set.
:::

### ICD-10 Code Redaction

Clinical notes sometimes contain the ICD-10 code itself — either embedded
in the note text by the EHR system or explicitly mentioned by the author.
A model that learns to detect these instances rather than the underlying
clinical signal would achieve artificially inflated training accuracy.

We apply a regex-based redaction step that identifies and replaces ICD-10
code patterns with a `[REDACTED]` marker before tokenisation. The pattern
matches both the dotted format (`M25.562`) and the raw format (`M25562`).

The redaction operates at both training and evaluation time, ensuring that
no code leakage is possible.

---

## Train/Validation/Test Splits {#sec-data-splits}

All experiments use a single set of **deterministic, stratified splits**
generated once and reused across all models. Using the same splits is
essential for valid comparison between experiments — if each experiment
generated its own splits, apparent performance differences could reflect
different test sets rather than model quality.

| Split | Records | Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| Train | 7,728 | 80% |
| Validation | 966 | 10% |
| Test | 966 | 10% |

: Train/validation/test split sizes. {#tbl-data-splits}

Splits are stratified by ICD-10 chapter to ensure proportional chapter
representation in each split. The random seed is fixed at 42 across all
experiments.

The test set of **966 records** is used for all final evaluation figures
reported in this document. The validation set is used only for checkpoint
selection during training (choosing the best epoch by validation Macro F1).

::: {.callout-warning}
## Test Set Integrity

The test set is never used for any training or hyperparameter selection
decision. All tuning is performed on the validation set. The test set is
treated as a final held-out evaluation that is touched only once per
experiment.
:::

---

## Data Pipeline Architecture {#sec-data-pipeline}

The data pipeline follows a Medallion architecture with three layers:

**Raw layer** — The original MedSynth CSV as downloaded from HuggingFace.
Registered in DuckDB for queryability but never modified.

**Silver layer** — Records validated against the CDC FY2026 code list,
with code status annotations (`billable`, `noisy`, `placeholder`). Stored
as Parquet.

**Gold layer** — APSO-flipped, ICD-10-redacted records ready for model
input, with the final `apso_note` column. This is the layer consumed by
all training and inference code. Stored as timestamped Parquet with a full
JSONL audit trail.

```
Raw CSV (HuggingFace)
    ↓ DuckDB registration
Silver Parquet (validated, annotated)
    ↓ APSO-Flip + ICD-10 redaction
Gold Parquet (model-ready)
    ↓ Deterministic splits (seed=42)
Train / Val / Test Parquet
```

All pipeline steps are logged to an append-only JSONL audit trail
(`outputs/evaluations/processing_log.jsonl`) with timestamps, record
counts, and SHA-256 hashes of input and output files.
